turns.content: I'm trying to pass a database connection pool into my handlers but it feels like I'm just passing around a raw pointer everywhere, which is the same mess I used to have in C++ with manual memory management.

That frustration comes from trying to manage lifetime and ownership manually instead of letting the runtime handle it. In Actix-web you wrap that pool in web::Data<T> which holds an Arc pointer so every handler gets a shared reference without copying. Think of it like a bank vault: your connection pool is the money, but you don't carry cash around; you give everyone access to the vault key through a secure window, and only someone with that key can withdraw.
So I just wrap my Arc<Pool> in web::Data and inject it into the handler? But what if a thread tries to mutate the pool directly while another is using it?

Exactly you should never mutate the pooled connection itself from inside handlers because they are just observers. If you need to change configuration like timeout or max size, create a new web::Data<T> variant and replace the old one via actix-web's config system. The Arc ensures all threads see the same snapshot at any moment, so no data races occur unless you're actually trying to modify the pool object which is generally wrong.
Got it but I'm still confused about how web::Data gets injected into the handler automatically. Is there a macro or attribute somewhere?

Yes, Actix-web uses the #[actix_web::main] and #[actix_web::route] macros along with ServiceBuilder to wire up your web::Data<T> automatically. The framework extracts the data from the service and passes it into your handler via environment context. You don't manually call any function like get_data or inject_state; you just declare your handler as async fn my_handler() -> Result<HttpResponse, E> and the compiler ensures web::Data<T> is available in its scope.
That sounds too convenient. What if I forget to wrap it in Arc and try to pass a simple Ref<Pool>? Will that break things silently or give me an immediate compile error?

It will give you an immediate compile error because Actix-web expects the data type to be Send + Sync so it can safely share across threads. If you pass a Ref<Pool> without Arc, the compiler complains that it's not 'Send' or 'Sync' depending on whether your pool has internal locks. The framework won't silently accept unsafe patterns; Rust's borrow checker will catch any ownership mismatch before runtime.

Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to inject a database pool into my handler, but the compiler complains about ownership and lifetimes. It seems like actix-web requires me to wrap everything in web::Data instead of just passing it around like a regular struct.

That's because Actix-Web's router uses request-scoped clones for parameters, so you need something that guarantees the data lives long enough and can be cloned safely. You wrap your database pool in Arc to ensure shared ownership across threads, then put that Arc inside web::Data<T>. When the handler is called, actix-web automatically provides an instance of the Data wrapper via a trait method on the request context, so you don't manually bind it.
Okay so I wrap my pool in web::Data and then call .get() inside the handler? But wait, if I'm using Arc already, why do I need another layer of indirection with Data?

You're correct that you can access the inner value via .get(), but the key distinction is that web::Data<T> implements Clone where plain Arc does not in a way that fits Actix-Web's request lifecycle. The Data wrapper ensures the pool remains valid for the entire duration of the request, even if multiple threads try to read from it concurrently. Without this layer, you risk dangling references or race conditions when the request object gets dropped.
